skill.name: generating-sorbet-inline
skill.description: Generate Sorbet inline type signatures for Ruby source files using sig blocks, typed sigils, and T::Sig conventions.
skill.summary: Adds Sorbet sig blocks directly to Ruby methods while preserving inline typing style.
skill.author: DmitryPogrebnoy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high and critical alerts, but most are false positives from Sorbet signature syntax, Markdown backticks, documentation URLs, and reference examples. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious network behavior was found. The remaining risk is moderate because the skill asks the agent to edit Ruby files and validate with an external Sorbet command.

medium_findings.title: External Sorbet Validation Command
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run `srb tc` or `bundle exec srb tc` to validate generated signatures. This is a legitimate Ruby typing workflow, but it invokes project tooling and should be performed only with user awareness in untrusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command guidance is explicit in the skill instructions. The command is not malicious by itself, but it is real external command execution in the target project context.

medium_findings.title: Reference Examples Contain Executable File Operations
medium_findings.description: The Packwerk reference examples include file writes and cache writes. These files appear to be examples for type-signature generation, not invoked by the skill, but they demonstrate filesystem-changing patterns that require review if copied into generated code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are present in reference code, but there is no evidence that the skill executes these files. The risk is contextual and depends on whether the examples influence generated code.

medium_findings.title: Reference Examples Include Dynamic Require Patterns
medium_findings.description: Reference code loads Ruby files based on configuration or application paths. This is normal for Packwerk examples, but dynamic require behavior can execute local Ruby code if reused without validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic require calls are clear, but they are in reference examples rather than the active skill instructions. This lowers confidence that they represent a direct marketplace risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Mostly Sorbet Syntax False Positives
low_findings.description: Many weak-cryptography findings point to Sorbet words such as `sig`, `signature`, and `T::Hash`, not cryptographic APIs. The only confirmed MD5 usage is a reference cache digest, not password storage, signing, or security-sensitive hashing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows the highlighted terms are type-signature documentation, and the MD5 call is used for cache keys. No evidence of credential hashing or security-sensitive cryptography was found.

low_findings.title: Network Alerts Are Documentation Links, Not Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The network findings are hardcoded documentation URLs and source links in Markdown or example output. No evidence found of HTTP requests, telemetry, credential upload, or hidden remote control behavior in the skill instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations contain URLs in documentation or user-facing messages. I did not find evidence that these URLs are fetched or that data is sent to them.

low_findings.title: Backtick Execution Alerts Are Markdown and Ruby Comments
low_findings.description: Most external-command findings come from Markdown code fences, inline backticks, or Ruby comments that describe commands. The active command instruction is tracked separately as a medium finding.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual inspection shows these alerts are not Ruby shell backtick execution sites. They are instructional text, Markdown fences, or comments.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks the agent to run Sorbet validation commands after editing signatures. This can be appropriate, but external commands should be treated as a user-approved action in untrusted projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and active in the skill workflow. Risk is moderate because it is a standard validation command rather than suspicious shell logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Ruby Loading in Reference Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference examples include `require` calls derived from configuration or application paths. These are not skill instructions, but the pattern can execute local Ruby code if copied without constraints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real in example files, but I did not find evidence that the skill loads or executes those examples.

content.value_statement: Ruby projects often lack precise inline type signatures, which makes Sorbet adoption slower. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code to add method-level sig blocks directly in source files.
actual_capabilities: Adds `# typed:` sigils when a Ruby file does not already define one.
Adds `extend T::Sig` before generating Sorbet `sig` blocks.
Generates method, initializer, and attribute signatures in inline Ruby syntax.
Preserves existing Sorbet strictness unless the user asks to change it.
Uses local Ruby context to reduce unnecessary `T.untyped` annotations.
Guides validation with `srb tc` or `bundle exec srb tc`.
limitations: It focuses on inline signatures and does not generate RBI files.
It may need user input when types cannot be inferred from code context.
It does not guarantee perfect types for metaprogrammed Ruby behavior.
Validation commands depend on a working Sorbet setup in the project.

use_cases.title: Annotate Existing Ruby Services
use_cases.description: Add Sorbet signatures to service objects, models, or plain Ruby classes while keeping the current file structure.

use_cases.title: Prepare Gradual Sorbet Adoption
use_cases.description: Introduce `# typed: true`, `extend T::Sig`, and method signatures in files that are entering the typed codebase.

use_cases.title: Review Signature Quality
use_cases.description: Check existing inline signatures for broad types, missing returns, and inconsistent parameter annotations.

prompt_templates.title: Add Basic Signatures
prompt_templates.prompt: Add Sorbet inline signatures to this Ruby file. Preserve existing behavior and explain any type assumptions before editing.

prompt_templates.title: Annotate Without Untyped
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate Sorbet sig blocks for these methods. Avoid `T.untyped` unless no reliable type can be inferred from the code.

prompt_templates.title: Preserve Strictness
prompt_templates.scenario: Work in an existing Sorbet project
prompt_templates.prompt: Add missing inline signatures while preserving the current `# typed:` level and existing Sorbet conventions in nearby files.

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Iterate
prompt_templates.prompt: Add inline Sorbet signatures, then run the project Sorbet checker if allowed. Use the errors to refine the signatures.

output_examples.input: A Ruby class with an initializer, attribute readers, and a string-returning instance method.
output_examples.output: The class receives a typed sigil if needed, `extend T::Sig`, and signatures for the initializer, readers, and method.

output_examples.input: A Ruby file that already has `# typed: strict` and several private helper methods.
output_examples.output: The existing strictness is preserved, and each public and private method receives an inline Sorbet signature.

output_examples.input: A method with unclear parameter types due to metaprogramming or indirect calls.
output_examples.output: The skill asks for more context or records the assumption instead of silently choosing a broad type.

best_practices: Start with a small file and validate before applying the same pattern widely.
Review inferred types against tests, callers, and existing Sorbet conventions.
Allow validation commands only after confirming the repository is trusted.
anti_patterns: Do not replace difficult types with `T.untyped` without documenting why.
Do not change a file from a lower typed level to a stricter level without user approval.
Do not generate RBI files when the requested workflow is inline signatures.

faq.question: Does this skill create RBI files?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for inline Sorbet signatures inside Ruby source files.

faq.question: Can it work with existing Sorbet files?
faq.answer: Yes. It preserves the existing typed sigil and follows nearby inline signature style.

faq.question: Does it run project code?
faq.answer: The skill says not to run Ruby application code. It may ask to run Sorbet validation commands with user approval.

faq.question: Will it use `T.untyped`?
faq.answer: It tries to avoid `T.untyped` and asks for more context when a precise type is unclear.

faq.question: What Ruby code does it annotate?
faq.answer: It focuses on methods, initializers, and attributes rather than local variables or intermediate expressions.

faq.question: Is this safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Review changes first and approve any validation command only when you trust the repository.
